schools.summary: Gill: no sacrifice of any sort was to have a blemish, typical of the unblemished and immaculate Lamb of God, who, without sin, offered himself without spot to God.

themes: Thou shalt not sacrifice unto the LORD... any bullock, or sheep, wherein is blemish... an abomination
the perfect offering honouring God
Christ the immaculate Lamb without spot (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: this was in any of their cities in the land of Canaan.
Keil & Delitzsch: man or woman that hath wrought wickedness in transgressing his covenant.

themes: man or woman, that hath wrought wickedness... in transgressing his covenant
idolatry the gravest crime
covenant-treason against the King

schools.summary: Gill, with the Targum: going after the evil imagination, lusting after other lovers, forsaking the true God and his worship.
Keil & Delitzsch: serving the sun, moon, or host of heaven, which God had not commanded.

themes: served other gods... either the sun, or moon, or any of the host of heaven
adoring the creature above the Creator
the works of God made a rival to him (Rom. 1:25)

schools.summary: Gill: a report of this kind was not to be neglected, yet not concluded on by hearsay; it was to be looked into and the witnesses thoroughly examined, the matter found true and certain.

themes: enquired diligently, and, behold, it be true, and the thing certain
justice resting on established fact
none condemned on rumour (Prov. 18:13)

schools.summary: Gill: this was after being brought before a court, tried, found guilty, and sentenced, then brought forth to execution and stoned with stones till dead.

themes: Then shalt thou bring forth that man or that woman... and shalt stone them with stones, till they die
punishment bound to lawful trial
the evil put away from the nation (1 Cor. 5:13)

schools.summary: Gill: two witnesses were sufficient, three the better, but on the testimony of one, sentence might not be pronounced.

themes: At the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, shall he... be put to death; but at the mouth of one witness... not
life guarded from single testimony
every matter established by witnesses (Matt. 18:16)

schools.summary: Gill, with Aben Ezra: they were to cast the first stone, a further trial of their testimony; their readiness showed their zeal for God's honour, and afterward all the people joined.

themes: The hands of the witnesses shall be first upon him... and afterward the hands of all the people
the rite testing the witnesses' sincerity
the community sharing in putting away evil

schools.summary: Gill: spoken to inferior judges in the country cities, who might have cases too wonderful or hidden, beyond their capacity.
Keil & Delitzsch: such matters carried up to the place the LORD should choose.

themes: If there arise a matter too hard for thee in judgment... get thee up into the place which the LORD... shall choose
a higher court for difficult cases
God's ordering of justice in degrees

schools.summary: Gill: the priests of the tribe of Levi; Maimonides observes the great sanhedrin should have priests and Levites in it, who should show the sentence of judgment.

themes: thou shalt come unto the priests the Levites, and unto the judge... and they shall shew thee the sentence of judgment
the supreme tribunal joining priest and judge
justice resting on the law of God

schools.summary: Gill: the inferior courts were to return and proceed according to the judgment of the great court at Jerusalem.
Wesley: the lower judge bound to follow the higher court's direction.

themes: thou shalt do according to the sentence, which they... shall shew thee
submission to the higher authority
the order and unity of justice (Heb. 13:17)

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to make any new law, but to teach the law of God; so far as their judgment agreed with that law they were to be regarded.
Keil & Delitzsch: the duty not to decline from their sentence, to the right hand or to the left.

themes: According to the sentence of the law which they shall teach thee... thou shalt not decline
authority bounded by the law of God
the ruling weighty as it declares his statutes (Mal. 2:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the country judge who, after applying to Jerusalem, is conceited in his own opinion, rejects theirs, and obstinately takes his own way.
Clarke: the man who acts presumptuously.

themes: the man that will do presumptuously, and will not hearken... even that man shall die
the contempt of lawful authority a grave offence
order guarded against rebellious self-will (Rom. 13:2)

schools.summary: Gill: all the people, and particularly the judges in their cities, would hear what was done to the obstinate elder and be afraid to commit the like.
Keil & Delitzsch: the deterrent — all the people shall hear and fear.

themes: And all the people shall hear, and fear, and do no more presumptuously
the just punishment of one a warning to many
the dread of penalty restraining sin (1 Tim. 5:20)

schools.summary: Gill: this concerns coming into the land of Canaan.
Henry: after the laws for subjects fitly follow laws concerning kings, for those that rule others must remember they are themselves under command.

themes: I will set a king over me, like as all the nations that are about me
the foreseen desire for a king
rulers themselves under command

schools.summary: Gill: not a command to set a king but a permission, in case they desired one, with the restriction to appoint none but whom God should choose, from among their brethren, not a stranger.

themes: Thou shalt... set him king over thee, whom the LORD thy God shall choose... not a stranger
the throne under God's appointment
the King chosen of God and made our brother (Heb. 2:17)

schools.summary: Gill: that he might not put his trust in outward things, as some trust in horses and chariots, nor oppress his subjects with a standing army, keeping no more horses than for his own use.

themes: he shall not multiply horses to himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt
the king not to trust in military might
safety in God, not in chariots (Ps. 20:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the multiplying of wives would turn his heart from the duty of his office and from religion, as Solomon's heart was turned by his idolatrous wives; he was not greatly to multiply silver and gold.

themes: Neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away: neither... silver and gold
guarding the king from lust and greed
the heart drawn from God by indulgence (1 Kings 11:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the Septuagint and Vulgate interpret 'this law' of the book of Deuteronomy, a summary of God's laws to the people.
Keil & Delitzsch: the king writing a copy from that before the priests the Levites.

themes: when he sitteth upon the throne... he shall write him a copy of this law in a book
the king putting himself under God's word
the ruler himself ruled by the law

schools.summary: Gill: it was to be with him always, and he was to read it every day, meditating on it night and day as a good man does, to learn the fear of the LORD and to keep all the words of the law.

themes: he shall read therein all the days of his life: that he may learn to fear the LORD his God
the godly ruler a daily student of the word
meditating in the law day and night (Ps. 1:2)

schools.summary: Gill: that his heart be not lifted up above his brethren on account of his office, considering he was subject to the law of God and accountable to him, and that he turn not aside, that his days be prolonged.

themes: That his heart be not lifted up above his brethren... that he may prolong his days in his kingdom
the throne established by humility and obedience
Christ the humble King exalted forever (Phil. 2:8-9)
